title: Suspicious USB Stick (BTLO)

headings.text: 1- What file is the autorun.inf running?
2- Does the pdf file pass virustotal scan? (No malicious results returned)
3- Does the file have the correct magic number?
4- What OS type can the file exploit? (Linux, MacOS, Windows, etc)
5- A Windows executable is mentioned in the pdf file, what is it?
6- How many suspicious /OpenAction elements does the file have?

overview: One of our clients informed us they recently suffered an employee data breach. As a startup company, they had a constrained budget allocated for security and employee training. study.whySoc: Use this note as an analyst reference: understand the concept, identify the relevant telemetry, pivot through evidence, and document the decision path clearly.
keyConcepts: 1- What file is the autorun.inf running?
2- Does the pdf file pass virustotal scan? (No malicious results returned)
3- Does the file have the correct magic number?
4- What OS type can the file exploit? (Linux, MacOS, Windows, etc)
5- A Windows executable is mentioned in the pdf file, what is it?
workflow: Identify the user, host, process, network flow, or identity object involved.
Collect the highest-value logs first and preserve timestamps and assumptions.
Pivot through related notes, telemetry sources, and MITRE references.
Document findings, gaps, containment options, and detection improvements.
tips: autorun.inf is a configuration file used in the root of removable drives. Windows Autoplay reads it to automatically execute a file when the drive is inserted. Attackers often use it to silently launch malicious files without user interaction. In this case, the USB stick was configured to open a PDF automatically.

/OpenAction is a PDF object that specifies an action to perform when the document is opened, such as launching a program or executing embedded JavaScript. It’s a common vector for malicious PDFs to automatically execute payloads.
